## PAGE 00136: Hardening Web Application Platform

- page_id: `227171547`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227171547/Hardening+Web+Application+Platform
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-03-09T12:40:55.490+01:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Hardening Teamwork Cloud and services
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

**Used scripts**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh']

The Web Application Platform, as deployed by our installer, runs on a bundled Apache Tomcat. As such, best practices for hardening Apache Tomcat should be followed.

Although we have already constrained ciphers and protocols at the JVM level, it is best practice to do so at the Tomcat configuration level. We also need to address issues such as secure cookies, disable XSS on foreign sites, and also remove default directories published as part of the default installation. The official Tomcat documentation covers a large portion of this (recommended reading) - [Tomcat Security Considerations](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-10.1-doc/security-howto.html). Additionally, there are a plethora of documents online covering all aspects of securing Tomcat to OWASP standards.

##### server.xml

Follow steps below to make changes to *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml.*

1. Disable the AJP connector unless you specifically intend to use it by doing the following: 
Replace the following lines: ]]> with the lines below: <Connector port="8009" protocol="AJP/1.3" redirectPort="8443" />]]>
2. Prevent the Tomcat instance from advertising what server is being used when an error is encountered by doing the hollowing:Add the following lines at the end of the file, right above the closing </Host> tag. ]]>

- The shutdown port 8005 is disabled by default. If you need to enable it, we recommend to change the default SHUTDOWN password. To enable the port 8005, open the <install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and do the following: Replace the following line: ]]> with the line below: ]]>

- The port 8080 with redirection is disabled by default. To enable it open the <install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and uncomment the following lines: -->]]>

##### web.xml

Having completed the configuration of server.xml, we will now proceed to configure *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/web.xml.*

We need to ensure that cookies are constrained to HTTPS.

```text
30
		
		true
		true
		
    ]]>
```

You only need to insert the following lines:

**<cookie-config>**

**<http-only>true</http-only>**

**<secure>true</secure>**

**</cookie-config>**

##### Tomcat installation

Having made the necessary changes to the configuration files, we will now proceed to remove all of the default applications in the tomcat distribution, which could expose our installation to external vulnerabilities.

If we look at a directory of *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/webapps*, we will see the following:

```text

```

As you can see, in addition to *webapp.war* and the *webapp* directory, there are additional directories, containing applications, which could potentially be exploited.

You want to remove *docs*, *examples*, *host-manager*, *manager*, and *ROOT*.

When you remove the ROOT application directory, accessing [https://ip_address:8443](https://ip_address:8443) will no longer display the Apache Tomcat default landing page.

##### Upgrading and patching Apache Tomcat

Web Application Platform uses Apache Tomcat as the Java web application server for deployment. Upgrading or patching is often required to keep your Tomcat server up to date with all the latest security and performance improvements.

Refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page for version compatibility between Web Application Platform and Apache Tomcat releases.

The files required for upgrading and patching Tomcat are a compilation of JAR files residing in*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/bin* and *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/lib*.

Always make a backup of these directories before performing any upgrade or patch.

For Linux server deployment, use the*upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh*script to automatically update your instance to the target Tomcat version (set in script). This script can perform installation either from a locally-stored package or pull from Apache’s server (internet access required).

To upgrade Apache Tomcat on Linux using script

1. Download *upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh* script.
2. For a server with internet access:
  1. Execute the script and specify the desired Tomcat version when prompted.

**For offline installation:**

1. 
  1. Download the Apache Tomcat package from https://archive.apache.org/dist/tomcat (tar.gz and zip files are both supported.)
  2. Execute the script and confirm installation from a local package.

To manually "slip-stream" an update without having to fully replace the Tomcat installation, replace the existing JAR files in these directories with ones from the newer Apache Tomcat package. After replacing the JAR files, make sure the new files’ permission and ownership are same as before.

##### Upgrading Web Application Platform bundled JRE

To automatically upgrade the JRE, download and run the *upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh* script. When prompted, provide the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''].

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227172776 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00137: How to approve requirements

- page_id: `227172776`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227172776/How+to+approve+requirements
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-05T15:29:54.681+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Editing and contextualizing > Model editing use cases
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

One of the ways you can benefit from editing your model in a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document is reviewing and approving Requirements. The following workflow will give you instructions how to do it:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='How to approve requirements' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] . You will need custom Requirements to be able to change their Status (e.g., to Approved).
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using server projects' space='MD2024xR3'] .
3. In the model, create and define Requirements of the custom Requirement subtype you have created. Converting default requirementsIf you already have default requirements defined in your model, you can convert them to the custom Requirement subtype by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Refactoring' space='MD2024xR3'].
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] (or the part of the model containing Requirements) to Cameo Collaborator for review and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sharing document links' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] with reviewers.
5. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='How to approve requirements' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] .

##### Creating a custom Requirement subtype

If you want to change Requirement status, you need to create a custom Requirement stereotype with the Status tag, as described below.

To create a custom Requirement subtype

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a new project' space='MD2024xR3'] .
2. In the project, Create a Profile element and name it Custom Stereotypes .
3. In the Custom Stereotypes Profile, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024xR3'] .
4. In the Profile Diagram, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024xR3'] and name it Custom Requirement .
5. In the Model Browser, find the Requirement [Class] element (it should be located in the SysML Profile) and drag it to the Profile Diagram.
6. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a relationship' space='MD2024xR3'] from the Custom Requirement to the Requirement [Class] .
7. In the same Profile Diagram, create an Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name it Status . [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_enumeration_instance.png']
8. Create three Enumeration Literals for the Status Enumeration instance, as displayed below, and name them In Progress , Approved , and Disapproved . [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_enumaration_literal.png']
9. Drag the Status Enumeration instance shape to the Custom Requirement Stereotype shape. Your Profile Diagram should look like the one shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='profile_diagram.png']

Now that you have created a profile with a custom Requirement subtype, you can use it in the model where you want to create custom Requirements.

##### Approving Requirements

To approve Requirements

1. Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Requirements you want to review. See the example of a Requirements table below. [ATTACHMENT filename='requirements_table.png']
2. Review the requirements and do one or several of the following actions:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing element values' space='TWCloud2024xR3']Change the **Status** value of the Requirements that do not need any changes to **Approved**. Empty values cannot be editedYou can change only existing Status values. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Status values (e.g., In Progress) are defined before [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space='TWCloud2024xR3'].
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] for items that need to be discussed.
3. Inform the author that you have finished reviewing the document.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Drag the <em>Status</em> Enumeration instance shape to the <em>Custom Requirement</em> Stereotype shape. Your Profile Diagram should look like the one shown below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="profile_diagram.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="How to approve requirements" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ol><p>Now that you have created a profile with a custom Requirement subtype, you can use it in the model where you want to create custom Requirements.<br /><br /></p><h3>Approving Requirements</h3><p><br /></p><p>To approve Requirements</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Requirements you want to review. See the example of a Requirements table below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="requirements_table.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="How to approve requirements" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Review the requirements and do one or several of the following actions:</span><br /><ul><li><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Editing element values" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:link-body>Change the <strong>Status</strong> value of the Requirements</ac:link-body></ac:link> that do not need any changes to <strong>Approved</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a68b17de-b140-4c57-8055-083a564eb5f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Empty values cannot be edited</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can change only existing Status values. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Status values (e.g., In Progress) are defined before <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Publishing documents" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[publishing a model for editing]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create textual or graphical comments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for items that need to be discussed.</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li></ul></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Inform the author that you have finished reviewing the document.</span></li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227172784 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00138: How to specify Interfaces

- page_id: `227172784`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227172784/How+to+specify+Interfaces
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-05-05T15:07:36.570+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Editing and contextualizing > Model editing use cases
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you are responsible for for a set of interfaces or an interface library, you can enter interface values (e.g. allowed voltages) via a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document.

To specify interface values

1. Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Interfaces you want to specify. See the sample table displaying the allowed voltages of Interfaces below. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one or several of the following actions:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing element values' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] (e.g., minimum and maximum voltages). Empty values cannot be editedYou can change only the existing values of Interfaces. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Interface values are defined before [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space='TWCloud2024xR3'].
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] for values that need to be discussed.
3. Inform the author that you have finished updating the model.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If you are responsible for for a set of interfaces or an interface library, you can enter interface values (e.g. allowed voltages) via a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document.</p><p><br /></p><p>To specify interface values</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Open the Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document with the Interfaces you want to specify. See the sample table displaying the allowed voltages of Interfaces below.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specifying_interfaces.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="How to specify Interfaces" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Do one or several of the following actions:</span><br /><ul><li><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Editing element values" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specify the values of Interfaces]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (e.g., minimum and maximum voltages).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a68b17de-b140-4c57-8055-083a564eb5f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Empty values cannot be edited</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can change only the existing values of Interfaces. Cameo Collaborator does not allow to enter property values that have not been specified. So make sure that certain Interface values are defined before <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Publishing documents" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[publishing a model for editing]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with comments in Cameo Collaborator" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create textual or graphical comments]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for values that need to be discussed.</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li></ul></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Inform the author that you have finished updating the model.</span></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227172460 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00139: Identifying suspicious usages

- page_id: `227172460`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227172460/Identifying+suspicious+usages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-05-05T15:07:03.994+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Resource Usage Map
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

In the Resource Usage Map application, you can detect three types of suspicious usages: inconsistent version usages, cyclic usages, and unused resources. In addition, you can filter and investigate suspicious usages by using the Filters pane. The sections below explain how to do that.

##### Inconsistent version usages

The inconsistent version usages filter allows you to see which resources use different versions of the same resource directly and through other used projects. You can investigate every inconsistent version usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.

To investigate an inconsistent version usage

1. In the Filters pane of a resource usage map, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the inconsistent version usages filter to see the list of all the inconsistent version usages in the map.
2. Select the inconsistent version usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.
3. Click the **Details** button on the top right corner of the inconsistent version usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As you can see in the example map below, *Car Structure library* directly uses version 20.0 of *MD_customization_for_SysML* but other resources used by *Car Structure library* use version 19.0 v9 of *MD_customization_for_SysML* (indirect usage).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of an inconsistent version usage map.

##### Cyclic usages

The cyclic usages filter allows you to see which resourcesuse themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop. You can investigate every cyclic usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.

To investigate a cyclic usage

1. In the Filters pane of a resource usage map, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] next to the cyclic usages filter to see the list of all the cyclic usages in the map.
2. Select the cyclic usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.
3. Click the **Details** button on the top right corner of the cyclic usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As you can see in the example map below, *MD_customization_for_SysML* uses itself by using *ISO-80000* which uses *QUDV_SysML 1.4* and *QUDV_SysML 1.4* uses *MD_customization_for_SysML* thus forming a usage loop.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of a cyclic usage map.

##### Unused resources

The unused resources filter allows you to see which resources are not used by other resources stored in the Teamwork Cloud repository. Thisfilter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Click the <strong>Details</strong> button on the top right corner of the inconsistent version usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="inconsistent_version_usage_details.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Identifying suspicious usages" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br />As you can see in the example map below, <em>Car Structure library</em> directly uses version 20.0 of <em>MD_customization_for_SysML</em> but other resources used by <em>Car Structure library</em> use version 19.0 v9 of <em>MD_customization_for_SysML</em> (indirect usage).</p><p><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="inconsisten_version_usages.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Identifying suspicious usages" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="line-height: 1.66667;letter-spacing: normal;text-align: center;">An example of an inconsistent version usage map.</h6><h3 style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Cyclic usages</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The cyclic usages filter allows you to see which resources </span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">use themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. You can investigate every cyclic usage displayed in your resource usage map as described below.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To investigate a cyclic usage</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In the Filters pane of a resource usage map, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="expand.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Identifying suspicious usages" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> next to the cyclic usages filter to see the list of all the cyclic usages in the map.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Select the cyclic usage you want to investigate. A detailed map of that usage is created in the content pane.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Click the <strong>Details</strong> button on the top right corner of the cyclic usage map, to see full information of the selected usage as illustrated in the following figure.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="cyclic_usage_details.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Identifying suspicious usages" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li></ol><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br />As you can see in the example map below, <em>MD_customization_for_SysML</em> uses itself by using <em>ISO-80000</em> which uses <em>QUDV_SysML 1.4</em> and <em>QUDV_SysML 1.4</em> uses <em>MD_customization_for_SysML</em> thus forming a usage loop.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="cyclic_usages.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Identifying suspicious usages" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="line-height: 1.66667;letter-spacing: normal;text-align: center;">An example of a cyclic usage map.</h6><h3 style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Unused resources</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The unused resources filter allows you to see which resources are not used by other resources stored in the Teamwork Cloud repository. This</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> filter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="unused_resources.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Identifying suspicious usages" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227171827 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00140: Importing users and groups

- page_id: `227171827`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227171827/Importing+users+and+groups
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-06-19T11:08:34.610+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Admin web applications > Users application
- labels: ['unrestored-unknown-attachment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can import external users and user groups from LDAP servers to Teamwork Cloud.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- To import users or user groups at least one LDAP directory must be added to Teamwork Cloud (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding and configuring LDAP server' space='TWCloud2024x']).
- Because of the LDAP export limit, you can only import 1000 users at once.
- It is not possible to import users or user groups with the names that already exist as internal.

To import LDAP users or user groups

1. In the Users application, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and select [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
2. Select the LDAP server from which you want to import users and user groups and click OK .
3. In the search bar of the LDAP server pane, enter the name of the user or user group you want to import. If you want to get the list of all users stored in the selected LDAP server, type the asterisk sign (*) in the search bar.
4. Select the check-boxes next to the users and user groups you want to import.
5. Optionally click the Set roles button and assign specific roles to the users and user groups to be imported. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding roles' space='TWCloud2024x'] .
6. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='import.jpg'] to start the import.

After completing the above steps, you will get a notification that the import process has started. If you import a large number of users at once, it may take a while before the import is finished.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Importing users and user groups in the Users application.

When imported users are new, external users are created and the selected roles are assigned to them (see step 5). If you import users that have been imported before, the existing user information is synchronized during the import process but the role assignments stay the same. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Synchronizing users and user groups' space='TWCloud2024x'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

ac:macro-id="98ca8cfd-64c6-41a3-ab0f-ef419be28c73"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To import users or user groups at least one LDAP directory must be added to Teamwork Cloud (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloud2024x" ri:content-title="Adding and configuring LDAP server" /></ac:link>).<br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Because of the </span>LDAP export limit, you <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">can only import 1000 users at once.</span></li><li>It is not possible to import users or user groups with the names that already exist as internal.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To import LDAP users or user groups</p><hr /><ol><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Users application, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="25"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_users.png" /></ac:image> and select <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import_users_and_groups.png" /></ac:image>.</span></p></li><li>Select the LDAP server from which you want to import users and user groups and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>In the search bar of the LDAP server pane, enter the name of the user or user group you want to import.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11e0cbf0-1d36-4c78-a0eb-da879dc20c47"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>If you want to get the list of all users stored in the selected LDAP server, type the asterisk sign (*) in the search bar.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Select the check-boxes next to the users and user groups you want to import.</li><li>Optionally click the <strong>Set roles</strong> button and assign specific roles to the users and user groups to be imported. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloud2024x" ri:content-title="Adding roles" /></ac:link>.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="25"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import.jpg" /></ac:image> to start the import.</li></ol><p><br />After completing the above steps, you will get a notification that the import process has started. If you import a large number of users at once, it may take a while before the import is finished.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:height="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="importing_users_and_groups.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Importing users and user groups in the Users application.</h6><p>When imported users are new, external users are created and the selected roles are assigned to them (see step 5). If you import users that have been imported before, the existing user information is synchronized during the import process but the role assignments stay the same. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloud2024x" ri:content-title="Synchronizing users and user groups" /></ac:link>.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227172296 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00141: Importing users and groups from LDAP

- page_id: `227172296`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227172296/Importing+users+and+groups+from+LDAP
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-05-05T15:06:39.177+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Admin web applications > Settings application > Managing LDAP servers
- labels: ['importing-ldap-users', 'importing-ldap-group', 'synchronizing-users', 'synchronizing-groups', 'copy-assignment-in-ldap-form', 'copy-assignment-while-importing', 'assign-role-while-importing']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

**5**

You can import users and groups after all LDAP server configuration is completed. Teamwork Cloud importing also works as a user synchronization mechanism. Existing users will only be synchronized and new users in the Teamwork Cloud Admin will be imported. You can see user data in the **Users application**before choosing to import users. This importing and synchronization mechanism applies to groups as well. Data, including full name, phone, email, and department, will be synchronized.

##### Importing and synchronizing users and groups

To import/synchronize users and groups

1. On the selected LDAP server card click the **Import** button. The **Import LDAP user/group** pane opens.
2. Query all users/groups or specific users/groups and select them.
3. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='import_ldap_users.jpg'] button. New users/groups will be imported or existing users/groups will be synchronized. The existing user information will be updated but the role assignment stays the same.

##### Managing roles

You can assign roles in the same pane, while selecting users and groups to be imported.

###### Assigning roles

To assign a role from one user/group to another

1. On the Import LDAP user/group pane, next to Role assignments , click Set Roles .
2. In the Set roles pane, type a role name in the search bar.
3. Select a role. If it is a custom role, specify scope.
4. Click the Save button to save the changes.

###### Copying assignments

To copy assignment(s) from one user/group to another

1. On the Import LDAP user/group pane, next to Role assignments , click Set Roles .
2. In the Set roles pane, type a username whose assignments you want to copy in the search bar.
3. Select a user/group. The assignments associated with the selected user/group will appear.
4. Click the Save button to save the changes. The selected assignments will be assigned to the target user/group.

##### Troubleshooting

If you cannot synchronize users and/or groups, check the **server.log** file for a message warning about illegal characters. For example:

```text

```

If you see a similar message, allow the use of the character in the **application.conf** file:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Teamwork Cloud importing also works as a user synchronization mechanism. Existing users will only be synchronized and new users in the Teamwork Cloud Admin will be imported. You can see user data in the <strong>Users application </strong>before choosing to import users. This importing and synchronization mechanism applies to groups as well. Data, including full name, phone, email, and department, will be synchronized. </p><h3>Importing and synchronizing users and groups</h3><p>To import/synchronize users and groups</p><hr /><ol><li><p>On the selected LDAP server card click the <strong>Import</strong> button. The <strong>Import LDAP user/group</strong> pane opens.</p></li><li>Query all users/groups or specific users/groups and select them. </li><li>Click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:width="27"><ri:attachment ri:filename="import_ldap_users.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="Importing users and groups from LDAP" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button. New users/groups will be imported or existing users/groups will be synchronized. The existing user information will be updated but the role assignment stays the same.</li></ol><h3>Managing roles</h3><p>You can assign roles in the same pane, while selecting users and groups to be imported.</p><h4>Assigning roles</h4><p>To assign a role <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">from one user/group to another</span></p><hr /><ol><li>On the <strong>Import LDAP user/group</strong> pane, next to <strong>Role assignments</strong>, click <strong>Set Roles</strong>. </li><li>In the <strong>Set roles</strong> pane, type a role name in the search bar.</li><li>Select a role. If it is a custom role, specify scope.</li><li>Click the <strong>Save</strong> button to save the changes. </li></ol><h4>Copying assignments</h4><p>To <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">copy assignment(s) from one user/group to another</span></p><hr /><ol><li>On the <strong>Import LDAP user/group</strong> pane, next to <strong>Role assignments</strong>, click <strong>Set Roles</strong>. </li><li>In the <strong>Set roles</strong> pane, type a username whose assignments you want to copy in the search bar.</li><li>Select a user/group. The assignments associated with the selected user/group will appear.</li><li>Click the <strong>Save</strong> button to save the changes. The selected assignments will be assigned to the target user/group.</li></ol><h3>Troubleshooting</h3><p>If you cannot synchronize users and/or groups, check the <strong>server.log</strong> file for a message warning about illegal characters. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8202c58b-505a-47d4-9c76-eb2114437c58"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[INFO 2023-10-17 01:00:01.111Z CN=Name, OU=Users Has illegal character [\\]. Cannot be imported]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you see a similar message, allow the use of the character in the <span><strong>application.conf</strong> file:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b268c43-749e-47fc-bcec-5359bd1b4603"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.ldap.allowBackSlashChar = true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227172817 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00142: Inserting content from another document

- page_id: `227172817`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227172817/Inserting+content+from+another+document
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-05-05T15:07:40.044+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Editing and contextualizing > Contextualizing Cameo Collaborator documents
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can include content, such as diagrams and tables, from one document into another. Keep the included content up-to-date by selecting to display its latest version or include thecurrentcontent version to make sure it will stay the same.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To add media to a Cameo Collaborator document:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing documents' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] .
- You need the Read Resources permission or Resource Reviewer role for the document.
- You need the Read Resources and Edit Resources permission or Resource Contributor role for the project with read-write permissions for the project branch.

To insert content from another Cameo Collaborator document

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opening a resource' space='TWCloud2024xR3'].
2. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the bottom right corner of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Content pane' space='TWCloud2024xR3'].
3. Click the **Insert from document** icon in the location of an open document section where you want to include content. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. In the open wizard, select the document from which you want to insert the content.
5. In the next step, navigate to and select the document section from which you want to insert the content.
6. Select the element of the section you want to insert. You can only insert tables and diagrams.
7. Select one of the following insert settings:
  - Display latest version - automatically updates the inserted content to the latest version when it becomes available.
  - Display current version (at the time when inserting) - always displays the content version that was available at the time of insertion. [ATTACHMENT filename='insert_settings.png']
8. Click the Add button.
9. Repeat steps 3 to 8 to insert more content or click [ATTACHMENT filename='cancel_editing.png'] on the bottom right corner of the content pane to exit the editing mode.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227171372 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=4 -->
## PAGE 00143: Installation on Linux

- page_id: `227171372`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227171372/Installation+on+Linux
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-03-04T15:27:09.619+01:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Teamwork Cloud and Services Installation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following sections describe the full deployment of Teamwork Cloud on a single Linux server. The deployment includes installation of third-party components: Apache Cassandra database and FlexNet license server. A set of installation scripts are provided to streamline the installation process. This type of all-in-one deployment should be used for testing and evaluation. For a performant system, we recommend distributing components and services on multiple servers.

The following Linux packages are required for each service. All these packages are automatically pulled from online repositories when using the included installation scripts. For offline installation, refer to the specific installation sections for more information on accessing these packages. Also refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='System requirements' space=''] section for version information.

| Teamwork Cloud |
| --- |
| Open JDK Java (see )Linux font package: dejavu-serif-fonts |
| Apache Cassandra |
| Open JDK Java (see )Cassandra and Cassandra ToolsLinux package: chkconfig |
| FlexNet 11.19 |
| Existing Java installation (see )Linux utility package: unzipFlexNet 64-bit Installer and UtilitiesCameo Daemon for FlexNet |

**Prior to installation:**

- Make sure that your hardware and software meet the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='System requirements' space=''].
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Preparing the operating system' space='']Prepare the Linux system for Cassandra database deployment.
- Download and extract the installation script package: *[ATTACHMENT filename='TWC_MCS_install_scripts_2024xRefresh3.zip']*
  - Extract the package in the same location as the installer .bin file
  - Add execute permission to all script files

The provided installation scripts are designed to streamline the installation process and automate system configuration. These scripts require remote access to official online repositories by default. Offline installation is possible if prerequisites are available locally on the system. For more information, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Teamwork Cloud FAQ' space=''].

To install and configure a working instance of Teamwork Cloud, follow the guides below:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Apache Cassandra' space=''] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing the FlexNet server' space=''] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Teamwork Cloud services' space=''] .

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Teamwork Cloud installation and configuration on a single Linux server.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227171382 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00144: Installation on Windows

- page_id: `227171382`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227171382/Installation+on+Windows
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-05T15:29:49.125+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Teamwork Cloud and Services Installation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Teamwork Cloud installation and configuration on a single Windows server.

This section provides instructions on how to install Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud on Windows. During the installation, the installer asks you to provide the required configuration values and creates a Windows service for you.

After completing the installation, make sure you grant access through the firewall to the required ports, as shown in the diagram above.

Once the installation is complete, you need to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying Teamwork Cloud license' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] to start working with Teamwork Cloud in MagicDraw and using Teamwork Cloud Admin.

To install and set up Teamwork Cloud on Windows, complete the following steps:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='FlexNet license server installation and licensing' space='IL2024xR3'] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Cassandra on Windows' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Teamwork Cloud and services on Windows' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] .
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Starting Teamwork Cloud on Windows' space='TWCloud2024xR3'] .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="teamwork_cloud_single_node_deployment.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Installation on Windows" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Teamwork Cloud installation and configuration on a single Windows server.</h6><p>This section provides instructions on how to install Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud on Windows. During the installation, the installer asks you to provide the required configuration values and creates a Windows service for you.</p><p>After completing the installation, make sure you grant access through the firewall to the required ports, as shown in the diagram above.</p><p>Once the installation is complete, you need to <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Applying Teamwork Cloud license" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[apply the Teamwork Cloud license]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> to start working with Teamwork Cloud in MagicDraw and using Teamwork Cloud Admin.</p><p><br /></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To install and set up Teamwork Cloud on Windows, complete the following steps:</span></p><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL2024xR3" ri:content-title="FlexNet license server installation and licensing" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install FlexNet license server]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Installing Cassandra on Windows" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install Cassandra on Windows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Installing Teamwork Cloud and services on Windows" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install Teamwork Cloud and services on Windows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Starting Teamwork Cloud on Windows" ri:space-key="TWCloud2024xR3" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Start Teamwork Cloud on Windows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227171588 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00145: Installation using scripts

- page_id: `227171588`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227171588/Installation+using+scripts
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-21T14:23:53.107+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Monitoring stack
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

5

**Downloads**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh3.zip']

Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves setting up a monitoring node and at least one telemetry node. The monitoring node consists of a database for storing metrics, JMX interface for communication with Teamwork Cloud services, and web interface for realtime data visualization. The telemetry node installs server agents for streaming metrics to the monitoring node. We recommend deploying the monitoring node on a dedicated server. Single-node deployment of the Monitoring Stack and Teamwork Cloud is possible, provided there is enough system resources for all components.

#### Installing Monitoring Stack

**Single-node deployment**

Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves downloading the package, running the installation scripts on all nodes, and configuring visualization.

1. Download and extract *[ATTACHMENT filename='monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh2.zip']*.
2. Add execute permission to all .sh files extracted, if they are not set.
3. Navigate to the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder.
4. Execute *install_monitoring_node.sh* inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).
5. Stop **twcloud**service.
6. Navigate to the *Telemetry-Node-Install* folder.
7. Execute *install_telemetry_node.sh.*
8. Restart **cassandra**service.
9. Start **twcloud**service.

**Multi-node deployment**

Use*install_monitoring_node.sh* to install the monitoring components and configure one telemetry node.

Then, use *add_twc_monitor.sh*to add each additional telemetry node. These telemetry nodes can be a Teamwork Cloud node or dedicated Cassandra node.

Finally, run *install_telemetry.sh* on each telemetry node.

1. Download and extract [ATTACHMENT filename='monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh3.zip'].
2. Transfer the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder to the monitoring node.
3. Navigate to the *Monitoring-Node-Install* folder.
4. Execute *install_monitoring_node.sh*inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).
  - Provide the address for one of the telemetry nodes during installation.
5. After installation script finishes, execute *add_twc_monitor.sh* to add additional telemetry nodes.
  - One additional telemetry node will be added with each execution of *add_twc_monitor.sh.*
6. Transfer the *Telemetry-Node-Install*folder to each telemetry node.
7. On each telemetry node:
  1. Navigate to the Telemetry-Node-Install folder.
  2. Stop **twcloud**service.
  3. Execute *install_telemetry_node.sh* inside this folder.
  4. Restart **cassandra**service.
  5. Start **twcloud**service.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c55cda40-d59f-4890-895a-6dc4f7152e4c">On this page</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="491e3dfc-2826-4288-bed7-beb129ea2612"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">5</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Downloads</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh3.zip" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves setting up a monitoring node and at least one telemetry node. The monitoring node consists of a database for storing metrics, JMX interface for communication with Teamwork Cloud services, and web interface for realtime data visualization. The telemetry node installs server agents for streaming metrics to the monitoring node. We recommend deploying the monitoring node on a dedicated server. Single-node deployment of the Monitoring Stack and Teamwork Cloud is possible, provided there is enough system resources for all components.</p><h2>Installing Monitoring Stack</h2><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Single-node deployment</strong></p><p>Installation of the Monitoring Stack involves downloading the package, running the installation scripts on all nodes, and configuring visualization.</p><ol><li><span>Download and extract <em><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh2.zip" /></ac:link></em>.</span></li><li>Add execute permission to all .sh files extracted, if they are not set.</li><li><span>Navigate to the <em>Monitoring-Node-Install</em> folder.</span></li><li><span>Execute <em>install_monitoring_node.sh</em> inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).</span></li><li><span>Stop <strong>twcloud </strong>service.</span></li><li><span>Navigate to the <em>Telemetry-Node-Install</em> folder.</span></li><li><span>Execute <em>install_telemetry_node.sh.</em></span></li><li><span>Restart <strong>cassandra </strong>service.</span></li><li><span>Start <strong>twcloud </strong>service.</span></li></ol><p><strong>Multi-node deployment</strong></p><p><span>Use<em> install_monitoring_node.sh</em> to install the monitoring components and configure one telemetry node.</span></p><p><span>Then, use <em>add_twc_monitor.sh </em>to add each additional telemetry node. These telemetry nodes can be a Teamwork Cloud node or dedicated Cassandra node.</span></p><p><span>Finally, run <em>install_telemetry.sh</em> on each telemetry node.</span></p><ol><li><span>Download and extract <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="monitoring-stack_2024xRefresh3.zip" /></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span>Transfer the <em>Monitoring-Node-Install</em> folder to the monitoring node.</span></li><li><span>Navigate to the <em>Monitoring-Node-Install</em> folder.</span></li><li><span>Execute <em>install_monitoring_node.sh </em>inside this folder (the script will require JSON and configuration files in the folder).</span><ul><li><span>Provide the address for one of the telemetry nodes during installation.</span></li></ul></li><li><span>After installation script finishes, execute <em>add_twc_monitor.sh</em> to add additional telemetry nodes.</span><ul><li><span>One additional telemetry node will be added with each execution of <em>add_twc_monitor.sh.</em></span></li></ul></li><li><span>Transfer the <em>Telemetry-Node-Install </em>folder to each telemetry node.</span></li><li><span>On each telemetry node:</span><ol><li><span>Navigate to the Telemetry-Node-Install folder.</span></li><li><span>Stop <strong>twcloud </strong>service.</span></li><li><span>Execute <em>install_telemetry_node.sh</em> inside this folder.</span></li><li><span>Restart <strong>cassandra </strong>service.</span></li><li><span>Start <strong>twcloud </strong>service.</span></li></ol></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=227171377 space=TWCloud2024xR3 version=7 -->
## PAGE 00146: Installing Apache Cassandra

- page_id: `227171377`
- space_key: `TWCloud2024xR3`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/TWCloud2024xR3/pages/227171377/Installing+Apache+Cassandra
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2026-04-16T09:19:51.749+02:00`
- ancestors: Teamwork Cloud and Services > Deployment Guide > Teamwork Cloud and Services Installation > Installation on Linux
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

Teamwork Cloud uses Apache Cassandra, an open-source NoSQL distributed database. Before installing Teamwork Cloud, please follow the steps below to install Apache Cassandra.

##### Prerequisites

- OpenJDK Java (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] for version information)
- Apache Cassandra RPMs
  - cassandra- <version_number> .noarch.rpm
  - cassandra-tools- <version_number> .noarch.rpm

- chkconfig package (for service startup on boot)

##### Installing with script

The script downloads and installs the necessary packages, Cassandra, and the Cassandra tools from the Apache Software Foundation repository, and creates the necessary firewall rules to allow proper operation both for a single node or a cluster installation. The script will also install Java 11 and set it as the default system Java.

The script can also be used for offline installation, download the prerequisite RPM packages and place them in the same location as the installation script. Manually install Java 11 and set it as the system default Java. Installation with Apache Cassandra RPM still uses the older System V init to automate service startup on boot. In order to enable the Cassandra service with systemctl, installation of the chkconfig package is needed.

To install Apache Cassandra

1. Install Apache Cassandra by executing the *install_cassandra<version_number>_ol_rhel.sh* installation script. Exampletrue_ol_rhel.sh]]>
2. Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
3. Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are "UN", indicating that the node status isUp, and its state isNormal. bash Wait for a few minutes until Cassandra starts for the first time before checking if it is running. If Cassandra has not started yet, you will get the error: "No nodes present in the cluster. Has this node finished starting up?" This means that you need to give Cassandra more time to start.
4. If Apache Cassandra is not running or if you used installation options other than the one described in this chapter, optionally .

##### Developing a backup strategy

Before deploying Teamwork Cloud and Apache Cassandra in a production environment, it is imperative to have a fully implemented backup strategy. The Cassandra database stores all project and user data associated with Teamwork Cloud. Review the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Backup and restore data procedures' space=''] document. Ensure that you test the entire backup and restore process before your deployment goes live to users.

During the backup process, user access to Cassandra should be suspended. Refer to the [Cassandra backup documentation](https://cassandra.apache.org/doc/stable/cassandra/operating/backups.html) for more information.

Improper backup procedure can lead to total data loss! For example, taking an image snapshot of the storage system while Cassandra is actively accepting read and write requests will result in unrecoverable data.

##### Configuring Apache Cassandra for Teamwork Cloud

If you used other installation options and not the provided script or if Apache Cassandra does not start, configure it as described below.

install_cassandra<version_number>_<os_version>.sh

To configure Apache Cassandra

1. Locate the*cassandra.yaml*file (default: /etc/cassandra/conf/cassandra.yaml) and open for editing.
2. Find the following parameters related to the Cassandra node IP address and communication settings, and change them as shown below: bashExample
  - seeds - a comma-delimited list containing all of the seeds in the Cassandra cluster. Since our cluster consists of a single node, it contains only one entry - our IP address.
  - listen address - the IP address that Cassandra uses to listen for connections.
  - broadcast_rcp_address - the IP address used to broadcast to other Cassandra nodes in the cluster. This parameter may be commented. In such case, remove "#" and make sure there are no leading spaces.
  - rcp_address - when set to to 0.0.0.0, Cassandra listens to rpc requests on all interfaces.
3. Find the following parameters that control thresholds to ensure that the data being sent is processed properly, and change them as shown below: bashExample
4. To ensure that the default commit log size is 8GB (recommended), uncomment the**commitlog_total_space_in_mb** parameter as show as below. bashExample Ensure that the partition where the commit log is installed has enough space to accommodate a commit log of 8GB.
5. To point the data to the appropriate locations, find the following parameters and change them as shown below: bashExample
6. Start Apache Cassandra by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue
7. Check if Apache Cassandra is running by executing the following command: bashDJangotrue If Apache Cassandra is running, you should receive the output displayed below. If the service is fully operational, the first 2 characters of the last line are "UN", indicating that the node status isUp, and its state isNormal. bashExample

##### Configuring Cassandra memory usage

If you did not use the installation script or want to increase the RAM usage by Cassandra, make the following changes. Otherwise, these configuration changes are set automatically by the Cassandra installation script.

Configuration files are located in */etc/cassandra/conf/*.

- By default, the maximum RAM usage for Cassandra is 8GB. To change the amount of RAM used by Cassandra, uncomment **-Xms4G (min)** and **-Xmx4G (max)** in the *jvm-server.options* file and specify their values.
- In the jvm11-server.options and jvm8-server.options files, comment all lines from"### CMS Settings" to "### G1 Settings".
- In the *jvm11-server.options* and *jvm8-server.options* files, uncomment the following lines:
- In the *jvm11-server.options* and *jvm8-server.options* files, uncomment the following lines and set the values to the physical CPU core count (the values of both parameters should be the same):
- In the jvm8-server.options file, comment all lines from "### GC logging options" to the end of the file.
- Synchronize CPU clocks on all Cassandra cluster nodes. Otherwise, you may encounter issues when creating an empty Cassandra cluster.
- When using cqlsh, use Python 3.6.0 or a later version. Python 2.7 series is no longer supported.
- In the logback.xml file, comment the "<appender-ref ref="ASYNCDEBUGLOG" />" line. This will increase Cassandra's performance by disabling the debug log.

##### Configuring Linux environment for Cassandra performance

If you install Teamwork Cloud using the *install_twc_mcs_centos_rhel.sh* script, Cassandra performance is tuned automatically. However, if you plan to use other installation options or if you need to set other parameters after running the script, you can do it manually as described in this section.

To improve Apache Cassandra performance

1. Locate the *sysctl.conf* file (default: /etc/sysctl.conf) and open for edit.
2. To configure the TCP settings, add the following tuning parameters to the file: bashExample
3. To apply the setting without rebooting, execute the following command: bashDJangotrue

For more information about tuning Linux, see [DSE 6.8 Administrator Guide](https://docs.datastax.com/en/dse/6.8/dse-admin/datastax_enterprise/config/configRecommendedSettings.html).

###### Using jemalloc memory allocator

The jemalloc memory allocator package can potentially improve Cassandra performance. Our installation script does not install jemalloc. The easiest way to install this optional package is to first install the epel-release package. You can then pull the latest jemalloc release from the EPEL repository. An older version jemalloc is available for direct download and install.

- EPEL package (optional, for pulling jemalloc package)

- jemalloc package (optional for performance)
